3.1.1.intuition: Setting up an Express app is like building a house: you need a solid foundation (project structure), plumbing and electrical run in the right order (middleware registration order), and rooms organized by purpose (feature-based modules) rather than by material type.

exercises.title: Bootstrap an Express + TypeScript Project
exercises.description: Create a new Express project from scratch with TypeScript. Set up tsconfig.json, create separate app.ts and server.ts files, add a health check endpoint that returns JSON with status and uptime, and configure nodemon for development.
exercises.hint: Use process.uptime() to get the server uptime in seconds. Remember to install @types/express for TypeScript support.

exercises.title: Feature-Based Module Structure
exercises.description: Refactor a flat Express app into a feature-based structure. Create modules for 'products' and 'categories', each with their own routes, controller, and service files. Wire them into app.ts using Express Router.
exercises.hint: Each module's routes file should export a Router instance. Import and mount them with app.use('/api/products', productRoutes).

exercises.title: Environment-Aware Startup
exercises.description: Modify your server.ts to read PORT from environment variables with a fallback default. Add a startup log that prints the environment (development/production), port, and Node.js version. Ensure the app gracefully handles SIGTERM signals.
exercises.hint: Use process.env.NODE_ENV for environment detection. Listen for process.on('SIGTERM', ...) and call server.close() for graceful shutdown.

problems.problem: req.body is undefined when sending POST requests
problems.cause: The express.json() middleware is not registered before the route handler, so Express cannot parse the JSON request body.
problems.solution: Add app.use(express.json()) before any routes that need to read req.body. Also ensure the client sends Content-Type: application/json header.

problems.problem: TypeScript errors: 'Cannot find module express'
problems.cause: The @types/express package is not installed, so TypeScript does not have type definitions for Express.
problems.solution: Run npm install -D @types/express @types/node. Also check that your tsconfig.json has 'esModuleInterop': true for proper import syntax.

problems.problem: Changes to code are not reflected without restarting the server
problems.cause: Node.js does not hot-reload by default. Running with ts-node or node directly requires manual restarts.
problems.solution: Use nodemon with ts-node (nodemon --exec ts-node src/server.ts) or tsx watch (tsx watch src/server.ts) for automatic restarts on file changes.

further_reading.title: Bulletproof Node.js Project Architecture
further_reading.note: Excellent deep dive into layered architecture for Express apps

further_reading.title: TypeScript Express Starter
further_reading.note: Boilerplate templates for Express + TypeScript projects

3.1.2.intuition: Routing in Express is like a mail sorting facility: each letter (request) has an address (URL path) and a type (HTTP method). The sorting machine (Express router) checks each bin (route) in order until it finds a match, then delivers the letter to the right handler.

exercises.title: Build a RESTful Resource
exercises.description: Create a complete set of routes for a 'books' resource: GET /books (list with pagination via query params), GET /books/:id (single book), POST /books (create), PUT /books/:id (update), DELETE /books/:id (delete). Store books in an in-memory array. Support ?genre= and ?author= query filters on the list endpoint.
exercises.hint: Use app.route('/books') to chain GET and POST, and app.route('/books/:id') to chain GET, PUT, and DELETE. Filter the array using Array.filter() based on query parameters.

exercises.title: Route Parameter Validation
exercises.description: Create a middleware function that validates route parameters are positive integers. Apply it to all routes that use :id parameters. If the ID is invalid, respond with a 400 error. Test with /users/abc, /users/-1, and /users/42.
exercises.hint: Create a reusable validateId middleware that calls parseInt on req.params.id and checks isNaN and > 0. Use next() to continue or res.status(400) to reject.

exercises.title: Query String Parser Utility
exercises.description: Write a utility function parsePagination(query) that takes req.query and returns a validated { page, limit, sort, order } object with defaults and bounds checking. Page minimum is 1, limit range is 1-100, sort defaults to 'createdAt', order must be 'asc' or 'desc'.
exercises.hint: Use Math.max and Math.min for bounds. Default values with || or nullish coalescing ??. Return an object with the parsed values.

exercises.title: Nested Resource Routes
exercises.description: Implement nested routes for a blog: GET /users/:userId/posts (all posts by user), POST /users/:userId/posts (create post for user), GET /users/:userId/posts/:postId (specific post). Ensure both userId and postId are validated as numbers.
exercises.hint: You can access multiple params with req.params.userId and req.params.postId. Create a validation middleware that checks all numeric params in the route.

problems.problem: A specific route like /users/me is being caught by /users/:id
problems.cause: Route order matters in Express. If /users/:id is defined before /users/me, the parameterized route matches first with id='me'.
problems.solution: Always define specific (static) routes before parameterized routes. Move app.get('/users/me', ...) above app.get('/users/:id', ...).

problems.problem: req.params.id is a string but you need a number
problems.cause: Express route parameters are always strings. There is no automatic type coercion.
problems.solution: Explicitly parse with parseInt(req.params.id, 10) or Number(req.params.id). Always validate the result with isNaN() before using it.

problems.problem: ERR_HTTP_HEADERS_SENT: Cannot set headers after they are sent
problems.cause: The route handler sends a response (e.g., res.json()) but does not return, so execution continues and tries to send another response.
problems.solution: Always use 'return' before res.json(), res.send(), etc. when there is code that follows: return res.status(400).json({ error: '...' });

further_reading.title: Express Route Tester
further_reading.note: Interactive tool for testing Express route matching patterns

3.1.3.intuition: Think of req and res as two halves of a conversation. req is the incoming letter — it carries the sender's address (URL), the message (body), and any special instructions (headers). res is your reply — you choose the tone (status code), attach notes (headers), and write your answer (body).

exercises.title: Request Inspector Endpoint
exercises.description: Create a GET /inspect endpoint that returns a JSON object containing: method, path, full URL, all query parameters, all headers, IP address, and the user-agent string. This is useful for debugging.
exercises.hint: Use req.method, req.path, req.originalUrl, req.query, req.headers, req.ip, and req.get('user-agent').

exercises.title: Content Negotiation Handler
exercises.description: Build a GET /api/info endpoint that returns data in different formats based on the Accept header. Return JSON for application/json, plain text for text/plain, and XML-like string for application/xml. Use res.format() to handle negotiation.
exercises.hint: res.format() accepts an object where keys are MIME types and values are handler functions. Include a 'default' key for unsupported types.

exercises.title: Typed Request Handlers
exercises.description: Create a POST /api/products endpoint with full TypeScript typing. Define interfaces for the request body (name, price, category), response body (product with id), and query parameters. Use Express generic types: Request<Params, ResBody, ReqBody, Query>.
exercises.hint: The Request generic order is: Request<ParamsDict, ResponseBody, RequestBody, QueryDict>. Use {} for unused type parameters.

problems.problem: req.body is undefined even though express.json() is registered
problems.cause: The client is not sending the Content-Type: application/json header, so Express does not parse the body as JSON.
problems.solution: Ensure the client sends the Content-Type header. In fetch/axios, this is usually automatic for JSON. For curl, add -H 'Content-Type: application/json'. You can also check with req.is('json').

problems.problem: res.json() throws 'Cannot set headers after they are sent to the client'
problems.cause: A response has already been sent earlier in the handler (or in middleware), and the code tries to send another response.
problems.solution: Use 'return' statements after sending responses in conditional branches. Check that middleware calling next() does not also send a response.

3.1.4.intuition: Express Router is like a phone extension system. The main switchboard (app) routes calls to departments (routers), and each department has its own internal directory. A caller dials the main number plus an extension, and the switchboard routes them to the right department where local rules apply.

exercises.title: Modularize a Monolithic App
exercises.description: Take an Express app with all routes in a single file (users CRUD, posts CRUD, comments CRUD) and refactor it into three separate router modules. Each module should have its own routes file and controller. Mount them all under /api in app.ts.
exercises.hint: Create a Router() in each module's routes file, define routes relative to the mount point (use '/' not '/api/users'), and export the router.

exercises.title: Nested Resources with mergeParams
exercises.description: Build nested routes: /api/courses/:courseId/lessons/:lessonId/comments. Create three routers with proper nesting. The comments router should have access to both courseId and lessonId from parent routers.
exercises.hint: Use Router({ mergeParams: true }) for child routers. Mount with parent.use('/:courseId/lessons', lessonRouter) and lesson.use('/:lessonId/comments', commentRouter).

exercises.title: API Version Router
exercises.description: Create a versioned API structure where /api/v1/users returns { version: 1, users: [...] } and /api/v2/users returns { version: 2, users: [...], total: N }. Use nested routers to organize the versions.
exercises.hint: Create separate routers for v1 and v2. Mount them under a parent API router: apiRouter.use('/v1', v1Router); apiRouter.use('/v2', v2Router); app.use('/api', apiRouter).

problems.problem: Child router cannot access parent route parameters (req.params.userId is undefined)
problems.cause: By default, Express routers have their own params scope. Child routers do not inherit params from parent routers.
problems.solution: Create the child router with Router({ mergeParams: true }). This merges parent params into the child's req.params.

problems.problem: Class controller method throws 'Cannot read property of undefined' for this.service
problems.cause: When passing a class method as a route handler (router.get('/', controller.method)), the 'this' context is lost.
problems.solution: Use .bind(): router.get('/', controller.method.bind(controller)). Or wrap in an arrow function: (req, res, next) => controller.method(req, res, next).

problems.problem: Router middleware runs on routes it should not affect
problems.cause: router.use(middleware) applies to all routes registered AFTER it in the same router. If registered too early, it affects public routes too.
problems.solution: Register public routes before the middleware, or apply middleware only to specific routes: router.get('/private', authMiddleware, handler).

3.1.5.intuition: Configuration is like the settings panel on a machine. The machine (your code) stays the same, but the settings (environment variables) change depending on where it is installed — the factory floor (development), the testing lab (staging), or the customer site (production).

exercises.title: Build a Validated Config Module
exercises.description: Create a configuration module using Zod that validates: PORT (number, default 3000), DATABASE_URL (required URL string), JWT_SECRET (required, minimum 32 chars), NODE_ENV (enum: development/production/test), and LOG_LEVEL (enum with default 'info'). The app should exit with a clear error message if validation fails.
exercises.hint: Use z.string().transform(Number) for PORT. Use safeParse and check result.success. Log result.error.format() for readable error messages.

exercises.title: Multi-Environment Configuration
exercises.description: Set up a config system that loads .env.development, .env.test, or .env.production based on NODE_ENV, with .env as a fallback. Create a .env.example template. Test by switching NODE_ENV and verifying different values load.
exercises.hint: Call dotenv.config() twice: first with the environment-specific path, then with no path for defaults. Earlier values are not overwritten.

exercises.title: Graceful Shutdown Handler
exercises.description: Implement a graceful shutdown for your Express server that listens for SIGTERM and SIGINT, stops accepting new connections, waits for in-flight requests to complete (with a timeout), closes database connections, and then exits.
exercises.hint: Use server.close(callback) to stop accepting connections. Set a setTimeout for forced exit. Chain cleanup operations (db disconnect, etc.) before calling process.exit(0).

problems.problem: Environment variables are undefined even though .env file exists
problems.cause: dotenv.config() was called after modules that read process.env, or the .env file is not in the project root directory.
problems.solution: Call dotenv.config() as the very first thing in your entry file, before any imports that read config. If .env is not in the root, pass the path: dotenv.config({ path: './config/.env' }).

problems.problem: Application works locally but fails in production with missing env vars
problems.cause: The .env file is not deployed to production (correctly), but the required environment variables are not set in the production environment.
problems.solution: Set environment variables through your deployment platform (Docker env, Kubernetes secrets, AWS Parameter Store). Use a .env.example file to document required variables. Validate at startup with Zod to catch this immediately.

problems.problem: Secrets appear in logs or error messages
problems.cause: Logging process.env or the full config object, or including secrets in error messages.
problems.solution: Never log the entire process.env or config object. Redact sensitive fields in logging. Use a config structure that separates public config from secrets.

further_reading.title: The Twelve-Factor App: Config
further_reading.note: The foundational methodology for environment-based configuration

3.2.1.intuition: Middleware is like an assembly line in a factory. Each station (middleware function) inspects or modifies the product (request) as it moves down the belt. A station can stamp it (add data), reject it (send error response), or wave it through (call next). The product only reaches the final station (route handler) if every station passes it along.

exercises.title: Request Timer Middleware
exercises.description: Write middleware that records the start time on the request object, then listens for the response 'finish' event to calculate and log the total request duration in milliseconds. Format: 'GET /api/users 200 42ms'.
exercises.hint: Use Date.now() to record start time. Listen with res.on('finish', () => { ... }) to calculate duration when the response completes.

exercises.title: Request ID Middleware
exercises.description: Create middleware that generates a unique request ID (using crypto.randomUUID()), attaches it to req.requestId, and adds it as an X-Request-Id response header. Downstream handlers should be able to access req.requestId.
exercises.hint: Import crypto from 'node:crypto'. Set the header with res.set('X-Request-Id', id). Extend the Express Request interface for TypeScript.

exercises.title: Async Handler Wrapper
exercises.description: Write an asyncHandler utility function that wraps async route handlers and automatically catches rejected promises, forwarding errors to next(). Test it with a route that throws an error and verify the error handler catches it.
exercises.hint: The wrapper returns a function (req, res, next) that calls the original function and chains .catch(next) on the returned promise.

exercises.title: Conditional Middleware
exercises.description: Create a middleware factory function skipFor(middleware, paths) that runs the given middleware for all routes EXCEPT those in the paths array. Use it to skip authentication for /health and /login.
exercises.hint: Return a new middleware function that checks if req.path is in the paths array. If yes, call next() directly. Otherwise, call middleware(req, res, next).

problems.problem: Request hangs and eventually times out
problems.cause: A middleware function does not call next() and does not send a response, so the request is stuck in the pipeline.
problems.solution: Ensure every middleware either calls next() to continue or sends a response (res.json(), res.send(), etc.). Add a timeout middleware as a safety net.

problems.problem: Error handler middleware is never called
problems.cause: The error handler does not have exactly four parameters (err, req, res, next), so Express treats it as regular middleware.
problems.solution: Error handling middleware MUST have four parameters: (err, req, res, next). Even if you do not use next, include it in the signature. TypeScript: (err: Error, req: Request, res: Response, next: NextFunction).

problems.problem: Async middleware errors crash the server instead of being handled
problems.cause: Express 4.x does not catch rejected promises from async middleware. An unhandled rejection occurs.
problems.solution: Wrap async middleware in try-catch and call next(error) in the catch block. Or use an asyncHandler wrapper: fn(req, res, next).catch(next).

3.2.2.intuition: Built-in middleware is like the standard equipment in a car. express.json() is the fuel injector that converts raw fuel (request bytes) into usable energy (parsed objects). express.static() is the trunk — a dedicated space for carrying cargo (files) that passengers (API routes) do not need to handle.

exercises.title: Configure Body Parsing for Multiple Content Types
exercises.description: Set up an Express app that handles JSON bodies (limit 1mb), URL-encoded form data (extended: true), and raw binary data for a /webhooks endpoint. Create test routes that echo back the parsed body for each type.
exercises.hint: Use express.json(), express.urlencoded(), and express.raw() with different type options. You can apply express.raw() only to the /webhooks path.

exercises.title: Static File Server with Caching
exercises.description: Configure express.static to serve a public directory with proper caching: HTML files should have no-cache, CSS/JS should have max-age of 1 year, and images should have max-age of 1 week. Use the setHeaders option.
exercises.hint: Check the file extension in the setHeaders callback using path.extname(filePath) or string methods. Set different Cache-Control headers based on the extension.

exercises.title: SPA with API Routes
exercises.description: Build an Express app that serves a React/Vue SPA from a build directory, has API routes under /api, and falls back to index.html for all non-API routes (for client-side routing). Ensure API 404s return JSON, not HTML.
exercises.hint: Order matters: static files first, then API routes, then the SPA fallback catch-all. The API 404 handler should be mounted on the /api router, not globally.

problems.problem: req.body is an empty object {} instead of the expected data
problems.cause: The Content-Type header does not match what express.json() expects, or the body is malformed JSON.
problems.solution: Verify the client sends Content-Type: application/json. Test with curl -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"key":"value"}'. Check for syntax errors in the JSON body.

problems.problem: 413 Payload Too Large error
problems.cause: The request body exceeds the limit set in express.json() or express.urlencoded(). The default limit is 100kb.
problems.solution: Increase the limit: express.json({ limit: '10mb' }). Set different limits for different routes if needed. For file uploads, use multer instead of body parsers.

further_reading.title: Express body-parser Migration Guide
further_reading.note: express.json() replaced the standalone body-parser package

3.2.3.intuition: These middleware packages are like the security and facilities team for an office building. Helmet is the security guard setting access rules at every door. CORS is the receptionist deciding which visitors from other companies can enter. Morgan is the front desk logging every visitor. Compression is the mail room that shrinks packages before sending them out.

exercises.title: Production-Ready Middleware Stack
exercises.description: Configure a complete middleware stack for a production Express API: helmet with API-appropriate settings, CORS allowing specific origins with credentials, morgan logging to both console and file, and compression with a 1KB threshold. Use environment variables for configuration.
exercises.hint: Create a writable stream for morgan file logging. Use different morgan formats for dev vs production. Read CORS_ORIGIN from process.env.

exercises.title: Dynamic CORS Configuration
exercises.description: Implement a CORS setup that reads allowed origins from a database or config file. The origin callback should check the incoming origin against the allowed list and reject unknown origins with a proper error.
exercises.hint: Use the origin option as a function: (origin, callback) => { ... }. The first argument to callback is an error (null for success), the second is a boolean.

exercises.title: Custom Morgan Tokens
exercises.description: Create custom morgan tokens for: request ID (from X-Request-Id header), response time in seconds (not ms), request body size, and authenticated user email. Create a custom format string using these tokens.
exercises.hint: Use morgan.token('name', (req, res) => value) to define custom tokens. Reference them in the format string as :name.

problems.problem: CORS errors in the browser: 'No Access-Control-Allow-Origin header'
problems.cause: The cors middleware is not configured, not matching the requesting origin, or the middleware is registered after the route.
problems.solution: Ensure cors() is registered before routes. Check that the origin option matches the frontend URL exactly (including protocol and port). Enable credentials if using cookies.

problems.problem: Helmet's Content-Security-Policy blocks API responses
problems.cause: Helmet sets a restrictive CSP by default that can interfere with API responses or frontend resources served by the same server.
problems.solution: For API-only servers, disable CSP: helmet({ contentSecurityPolicy: false }). For servers that also serve frontend, customize the directives to allow your resources.

problems.problem: Compression not working — responses are not compressed
problems.cause: The client does not send Accept-Encoding: gzip/deflate, the response is too small (below threshold), or the content type is not compressible.
problems.solution: Check that the client sends Accept-Encoding header (browsers do this automatically). Lower the threshold for testing. Verify the content type is text-based (JSON, HTML, etc.).

further_reading.title: MDN: Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS)
further_reading.note: Essential reading for understanding CORS mechanics

3.2.4.intuition: Writing custom middleware is like designing customs checkpoints at a border. Each checkpoint has a specific job — check passports (authentication), verify visas (authorization), scan luggage (validation). You design the rules, and every traveler (request) must pass through them in order.

exercises.title: Rate Limiter with Sliding Window
exercises.description: Implement a rate limiting middleware factory that uses a sliding window algorithm. It should accept options for windowMs, max requests, and an optional key generator function. Return appropriate 429 responses with Retry-After headers.
exercises.hint: Store timestamps of requests per key in an array. On each request, filter out timestamps older than windowMs. If the remaining count exceeds max, reject the request.

exercises.title: Request Validation Middleware with Zod
exercises.description: Create a validate() middleware factory that accepts Zod schemas for body, query, and params. It should validate all three, collect errors, and return a structured 400 error response. On success, replace req.body/query/params with the parsed (and coerced) values.
exercises.hint: Use schema.safeParse() to validate without throwing. Map error.errors to readable messages. Use result.data as the sanitized value.

exercises.title: Audit Log Middleware
exercises.description: Write middleware that logs write operations (POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE) to an in-memory audit log with: timestamp, user ID, method, path, request body, and response status code. Expose a GET /audit endpoint to view the log.
exercises.hint: Use res.on('finish', ...) to capture the response status code after the handler completes. Only log if req.method is not GET or HEAD.

exercises.title: Circuit Breaker Middleware
exercises.description: Implement a simple circuit breaker middleware that tracks failures for downstream service calls. After 5 consecutive failures, the circuit opens and rejects requests with 503 for 30 seconds before allowing a single test request through.
exercises.hint: Use a closure to maintain state: failure count, circuit state (closed/open/half-open), and last failure time. Reset on success.

problems.problem: Middleware runs but next handler never receives the modified request data
problems.cause: The middleware assigns to a local variable instead of modifying req directly, or assigns to a property that downstream code is not reading.
problems.solution: Assign directly to req: req.user = userData. Ensure TypeScript declarations extend the Express Request interface. Verify downstream code reads the same property name.

problems.problem: Middleware factory creates shared state between requests
problems.cause: Variables declared outside the returned middleware function are shared across all requests (closures). This is intentional for caches/counters but problematic for per-request state.
problems.solution: For per-request state, assign to the req object inside the middleware. For shared state (rate limiting), use the closure intentionally with proper cleanup.

further_reading.title: Zod Documentation
further_reading.note: Used for request validation middleware

further_reading.title: Express Async Errors
further_reading.note: Library that patches Express to handle async errors automatically

3.2.5.intuition: Error handling middleware is like the safety net under a trapeze act. No matter where a performer (route handler) falls, the net (error handler) catches them. But you must position the net below all the performers (register after routes), and it must be wide enough to catch everything (handle all error types).

exercises.title: Custom Error Hierarchy
exercises.description: Create a set of custom error classes: ApiError (base), NotFoundError, ValidationError, UnauthorizedError, ForbiddenError, and ConflictError. Each should set the appropriate HTTP status code. Write a global error handler that uses instanceof to format appropriate responses.
exercises.hint: Extend the built-in Error class. Use Error.captureStackTrace(this, this.constructor) for clean stack traces. The error handler should check instanceof ApiError first.

exercises.title: Async Error Wrapper
exercises.description: Write an asyncHandler utility that wraps async route handlers and catches rejected promises. Apply it to 5 routes that perform async operations. Verify that thrown errors reach the global error handler.
exercises.hint: The wrapper returns (req, res, next) => Promise.resolve(fn(req, res, next)).catch(next). Test by making a route throw an error and checking the response.

exercises.title: Environment-Aware Error Responses
exercises.description: Build an error handler that returns detailed error information (message, stack, request details) in development but returns only safe, generic messages in production. Include request ID in all error responses for correlation.
exercises.hint: Check process.env.NODE_ENV to determine detail level. Always include the request ID (from middleware or X-Request-Id header). Never expose stack traces or internal error messages in production.

problems.problem: Error handler middleware is never triggered
problems.cause: The error handler has 3 parameters instead of 4, or it is registered before routes, or async errors are not being forwarded with next(error).
problems.solution: Ensure the error handler has exactly 4 parameters: (err, req, res, next). Register it after all routes and 404 handlers. Wrap async handlers with try-catch and call next(error).

problems.problem: Stack trace and internal details exposed to users in production
problems.cause: The error handler sends err.message and err.stack regardless of environment.
problems.solution: Check NODE_ENV and only include detailed error information in development. In production, return a generic 'Internal server error' message for 500 errors.

problems.problem: Express crashes on unhandled promise rejection instead of returning a 500 error
problems.cause: An async route handler rejects without try-catch, and Express 4 does not catch async errors.
problems.solution: Use an asyncHandler wrapper for all async route handlers. Also add process.on('unhandledRejection', ...) as a safety net. Consider upgrading to Express 5 which handles this natively.

further_reading.title: express-async-errors Package
further_reading.note: Monkey-patches Express to catch async errors automatically

further_reading.title: RFC 7807: Problem Details for HTTP APIs
further_reading.note: Standard format for error responses

3.3.1.intuition: A REST API is like a well-organized library. Each book (resource) has a unique catalog number (URI). You can browse the catalog (GET collection), check out a specific book (GET by ID), donate a new book (POST), replace a damaged copy (PUT), add a bookmark (PATCH), or remove a book (DELETE). The librarian (server) does not remember who you are between visits (stateless) — you show your library card (auth token) every time.

exercises.title: Design a REST API for an E-Commerce Platform
exercises.description: Design the URL structure for an e-commerce API with resources: products, categories, users, orders, and reviews. Define the HTTP methods and expected responses for each endpoint. Include relationships (a product belongs to a category, an order has many items).
exercises.hint: Start with the main resources: /api/products, /api/categories. Use nesting for tight relationships: /api/products/:id/reviews. Use query params for loose relationships: /api/orders?userId=42.

exercises.title: Implement a Complete CRUD Router
exercises.description: Build an Express router for a 'tasks' resource with all CRUD operations, proper status codes, input validation, and consistent response envelopes. Store tasks in memory. Support filtering by status (?status=done) and sorting (?sort=dueDate&order=asc).
exercises.hint: Use 200 for reads, 201 for creates, 204 for deletes. Wrap responses in { success: true, data: ... }. Parse and validate query parameters for filtering.

exercises.title: PUT vs PATCH Implementation
exercises.description: Implement both PUT and PATCH for a user resource. PUT should require all fields and replace the entire object. PATCH should accept partial updates and merge with existing data. Write tests that demonstrate the difference.
exercises.hint: For PUT, validate that all required fields are present. For PATCH, use Object.assign() or spread to merge. PUT with missing fields should return 400.

problems.problem: API feels awkward — many endpoints have verbs in URLs
problems.cause: Thinking in terms of actions rather than resources. The developer is mapping function names to URLs instead of using HTTP methods.
problems.solution: Reframe: instead of POST /createUser, think 'I am creating a new user resource' -> POST /users. The HTTP method IS the verb. Resource URLs should be nouns.

problems.problem: Deeply nested URLs become unwieldy (/users/42/posts/7/comments/3/likes)
problems.cause: Over-nesting resources to represent every relationship in the URL hierarchy.
problems.solution: Limit nesting to 2 levels maximum. Use query parameters for deeper relationships: GET /likes?commentId=3. Or provide a direct route: GET /comments/3/likes.

further_reading.title: Fielding's Dissertation: REST
further_reading.note: The original definition of REST

further_reading.title: Microsoft REST API Guidelines
further_reading.note: Enterprise-grade API design guidelines

further_reading.title: JSON:API Specification
further_reading.note: A specification for building APIs in JSON

3.3.2.intuition: HTTP is a conversation with rules. The method is what you want to do (read, create, update, delete), the status code is the answer (success, your fault, my fault), and headers are the metadata — like the tone of voice, expiration dates, and formatting preferences in a business letter.

exercises.title: Status Code Decision Handler
exercises.description: Implement a complete set of CRUD routes for a 'projects' resource that uses correct status codes for every scenario: 200 for successful reads, 201 for creates with Location header, 204 for deletes, 404 for missing resources, 400 for validation errors, 409 for duplicate names.
exercises.hint: For 201 responses, set the Location header: res.set('Location', /api/projects/${id}). For 204, use res.status(204).send() with no body.

exercises.title: ETag-Based Conditional Requests
exercises.description: Implement ETag support for a resource endpoint. Generate ETags from resource data, check If-None-Match headers on GET requests (return 304), and check If-Match headers on PUT/PATCH requests (return 412 Precondition Failed if mismatched).
exercises.hint: Generate ETag from a hash of the resource data or updatedAt timestamp. Compare with req.get('If-None-Match') for GET and req.get('If-Match') for PUT/PATCH.

exercises.title: Content Negotiation Endpoint
exercises.description: Build a GET /api/reports/:id endpoint that returns data in JSON, CSV, or XML format based on the Accept header. Use res.format() for negotiation. Return 406 Not Acceptable for unsupported formats.
exercises.hint: Use res.format({ 'application/json': fn, 'text/csv': fn, 'application/xml': fn, default: fn }).

problems.problem: Client receives 200 OK but the operation actually failed
problems.cause: The API returns 200 for all responses, embedding the actual status in the response body. This breaks HTTP semantics and confuses clients.
problems.solution: Use proper HTTP status codes. 4xx for client errors, 5xx for server errors. HTTP clients and tools rely on status codes for error detection.

problems.problem: PUT request fails with 'Missing required fields' even though only one field changed
problems.cause: PUT replaces the entire resource. Unlike PATCH, it requires the complete representation.
problems.solution: Use PATCH for partial updates. If using PUT, the client must send all fields. Document this clearly in your API documentation.

further_reading.title: httpstatuses.com
further_reading.note: Quick reference for HTTP status codes with descriptions

3.3.3.intuition: Validation is like airport security. Every piece of luggage (request) goes through the same scanners (schemas) before entering the terminal (route handlers). If something is wrong, you get a clear, specific explanation of what failed and why — not just a vague 'access denied.'

exercises.title: Implement RFC 7807 Error Responses
exercises.description: Create an error handling system that returns RFC 7807 Problem Details format. Build custom error classes that produce compliant responses with type URIs, titles, status codes, and detail messages. Include validation error extensions.
exercises.hint: Set Content-Type to 'application/problem+json'. The 'type' field should be a URI (can be a URL to your error documentation). Include an 'errors' extension field for validation details.

exercises.title: Complete Zod Validation Pipeline
exercises.description: Build a validation middleware system with Zod schemas for a blog API: create/update post (title, content, tags, publishedAt), query parameters for listing (pagination, filtering by tag, date range), and route parameters (numeric ID). Include cross-field validation.
exercises.hint: Use z.string().transform(Number) for query params that should be numbers. Use .refine() for cross-field validation like date ranges. Use .partial() for update schemas.

exercises.title: Error Code Registry
exercises.description: Create an error code system where each error has a unique code (USER_NOT_FOUND, DUPLICATE_EMAIL, INVALID_TOKEN, etc.). Map these codes to status codes and default messages. Build middleware that translates internal errors (Prisma, JWT) to your error codes.
exercises.hint: Create a Map or object that maps error codes to { statusCode, message } pairs. Create a factory function: ApiError.fromCode('DUPLICATE_EMAIL', { email: 'a@b.com' }).

problems.problem: Validation errors show cryptic internal messages instead of user-friendly ones
problems.cause: Using default error messages from the validation library without customizing them.
problems.solution: Always provide custom error messages in Zod schemas: z.string().min(2, 'Name must be at least 2 characters'). Map technical field names to user-friendly labels.

problems.problem: Client cannot distinguish between different error types programmatically
problems.cause: All errors return the same format with only a human-readable message. No machine-readable error codes.
problems.solution: Include a 'code' field (e.g., 'VALIDATION_ERROR', 'RESOURCE_NOT_FOUND', 'DUPLICATE_RESOURCE') that clients can switch on. Consistent codes enable programmatic error handling.

further_reading.title: RFC 7807: Problem Details for HTTP APIs
Express Validator
further_reading.note: Alternative validation library built specifically for Express

3.4.1.intuition: Passport.js is like a security checkpoint at a building. The strategy is the type of ID check (badge, fingerprint, key card). When you pass, you get a visitor badge (session cookie) that lets you move freely through the building without showing ID again — until the badge expires.

exercises.title: Implement Local Auth with Sessions
exercises.description: Build a complete auth system with Passport Local Strategy: register, login, logout, and get-current-user endpoints. Hash passwords with bcrypt, manage sessions with express-session, and create isAuthenticated middleware.
exercises.hint: Remember the middleware order: express.json() -> session() -> passport.initialize() -> passport.session(). Use bcrypt.hash(password, 12) for registration and bcrypt.compare() for login.

exercises.title: Account Lockout System
exercises.description: Extend the login system to lock accounts after 5 failed login attempts within 15 minutes. Store attempt counts and lockout timestamps. Return appropriate error messages and remaining lockout time.
exercises.hint: Track failed attempts per email in a Map or database. Check lockout status before authenticating. Reset the counter on successful login.

exercises.title: Session Store with Redis
exercises.description: Replace the default in-memory session store with Redis using connect-redis. Configure proper TTL, handle Redis connection errors gracefully, and verify sessions survive server restarts.
exercises.hint: Install connect-redis and redis packages. Create a Redis client, pass it to RedisStore, and use that as the session store. Handle the 'error' event on the Redis client.

problems.problem: req.user is undefined after successful login
problems.cause: Session middleware is not configured, or passport.session() is not registered, or serializeUser/deserializeUser are not defined.
problems.solution: Ensure session middleware is registered before passport middleware. Implement both serializeUser and deserializeUser. Check that the session cookie is being set (look for connect.sid in browser cookies).

problems.problem: Sessions are lost on server restart
problems.cause: Using the default in-memory session store, which loses all data when the process exits.
problems.solution: Use a persistent session store like connect-redis, connect-pg-simple, or connect-mongo for production environments.

problems.problem: passport.authenticate callback not firing
problems.cause: Missing (req, res, next) call at the end of the custom callback pattern.
problems.solution: When using the custom callback pattern, passport.authenticate returns a middleware function that must be invoked: passport.authenticate('local', callback)(req, res, next).

3.4.2.intuition: A JWT is like a concert wristband. Once the bouncer (server) gives you the wristband (token), you can show it at any stage (service) without going back to the box office. But if the wristband is stolen, it works until the concert ends (expiry) — you cannot cancel a wristband that is already on someone's wrist.

exercises.title: Complete JWT Auth System
exercises.description: Implement JWT auth with login, register, refresh, and logout. Use access tokens (15min) and refresh tokens (7 days). Store refresh tokens in a database. Implement token rotation on refresh.
exercises.hint: Use different secrets for access and refresh tokens. On refresh, validate the stored refresh token, generate a new pair, and replace the stored refresh token.

exercises.title: Token Revocation with Redis
exercises.description: Add a token blocklist using Redis. When a user logs out or changes password, add their current access token to the blocklist with TTL matching the remaining token lifetime. Check the blocklist in the auth middleware.
exercises.hint: Calculate remaining TTL from the token's exp claim: Math.ceil(payload.exp - Date.now()/1000). Use Redis SET with EX option.

exercises.title: Role-Based Access Control
exercises.description: Build authorization middleware that works with JWT. Implement roles (admin, editor, user) and permissions. Create a middleware factory: authorize('admin', 'editor') that checks the role from the JWT payload.
exercises.hint: The authorize factory returns middleware that checks req.user.role against the allowed roles array. Chain with authenticate: router.delete('/users/:id', authenticate, authorize('admin'), handler).

problems.problem: JWT token verification fails with 'invalid signature'
problems.cause: The secret used to verify differs from the one used to sign, or the token was modified/corrupted.
problems.solution: Ensure the same secret is used for signing and verification. Check for extra whitespace or encoding issues in the secret. Use different secrets for access and refresh tokens but be consistent.

problems.problem: User stays logged in even after changing password
problems.cause: Existing JWTs remain valid until they expire. Password change does not invalidate outstanding tokens.
problems.solution: Implement token versioning: increment a version counter on password change and include it in the JWT. Reject tokens with outdated versions. Or use a blocklist to revoke all existing tokens.

problems.problem: Token expired errors flood the client
problems.cause: The client does not implement automatic token refresh, so every request after expiry fails.
problems.solution: Implement an HTTP interceptor (axios/fetch) that catches 401 TOKEN_EXPIRED errors, calls the refresh endpoint, and retries the original request with the new token.

further_reading.title: OWASP JWT Cheat Sheet
further_reading.note: Security recommendations for JWT implementations

3.4.3.intuition: OAuth2 is like checking into a hotel with your passport. Instead of the hotel (your app) running its own ID verification system, it trusts the government (Google/GitHub) to vouch for you. The hotel just needs to verify the passport is real and record your details.

exercises.title: Google OAuth Integration
exercises.description: Set up Google OAuth2 with Passport.js. Register your app in Google Cloud Console, configure the strategy, create login/callback routes, and handle user creation for new Google users. Use sessions for auth state.
exercises.hint: Set up credentials in Google Cloud Console under APIs & Services > Credentials. The callback URL must match exactly. Use passport.authenticate('google') for both the login route and the callback route.

exercises.title: Multi-Provider Auth
exercises.description: Extend your auth system to support both Google and GitHub OAuth alongside local (email/password) authentication. Handle the case where a user registers with one method and later wants to use another with the same email.
exercises.hint: Add a 'provider' column to your users table. Check for existing users by email during OAuth callback. If found with a different provider, offer to link accounts.

exercises.title: OAuth + JWT for SPAs
exercises.description: Implement OAuth authentication that returns JWTs instead of using sessions. After successful OAuth callback, generate access and refresh tokens and redirect to the frontend with tokens in URL parameters or set them as httpOnly cookies.
exercises.hint: Use passport.authenticate('google', { session: false }) to skip session creation. Generate tokens in the callback handler and redirect to frontend.

problems.problem: OAuth callback returns 'redirect_uri_mismatch' error
problems.cause: The callback URL in your code does not exactly match the one registered in the OAuth provider's console.
problems.solution: Check that protocol (http vs https), hostname, port, and path match exactly. In development, use http://localhost:3000. In production, use your actual domain with https.

problems.problem: User email is undefined from OAuth profile
problems.cause: The email scope was not requested, or the provider does not guarantee email in the profile.
problems.solution: Request the email scope explicitly: scope: ['email', 'profile']. For GitHub, emails may need a separate API call. Always handle the case where email is undefined.

problems.problem: CORS errors when redirecting to OAuth provider from SPA
problems.cause: Trying to initiate OAuth from a frontend fetch/axios call instead of a full page redirect.
problems.solution: OAuth requires a full browser redirect, not an AJAX call. Use window.location.href = '/auth/google' or an anchor tag. The server then redirects to Google's auth page.

further_reading.title: OAuth 2.0 Simplified
further_reading.note: Excellent plain-English explanation of OAuth2 flows

3.5.1.intuition: Prisma with Express is like having a perfectly organized filing system. Prisma is the filing clerk who knows exactly where every document is (type-safe queries), ensures documents follow the rules (schema validation), and handles complex filing tasks atomically (transactions). Express is the front desk that takes requests from visitors.

exercises.title: CRUD API with Prisma
exercises.description: Build a complete REST API for a blog with Prisma: users, posts, and tags. Implement all CRUD operations with proper error handling, pagination, and filtering. Use Prisma's relation queries for eager loading.
exercises.hint: Use Prisma's include for relation loading. Use skip and take for pagination. Handle P2002 (unique constraint) and P2025 (not found) errors specifically.

exercises.title: Prisma Transactions
exercises.description: Implement a 'publish post' feature that atomically: validates the post exists, sets published=true, creates a notification record, and updates the author's post count. Use Prisma's interactive transaction.
exercises.hint: Use prisma.$transaction(async (tx) => { ... }). All operations inside use the tx client, not the global prisma. If any operation fails, all are rolled back.

exercises.title: Advanced Filtering and Search
exercises.description: Build a search endpoint that supports: text search across title and content, filtering by multiple tags (AND/OR), date range filters, author filter, and published status. Construct dynamic Prisma where clauses.
exercises.hint: Build the where object conditionally using spread: { ...(search && { OR: [...] }), ...(tags && { tags: { some: { name: { in: tags } } } }) }.

problems.problem: PrismaClientInitializationError: Can't reach database server
problems.cause: The DATABASE_URL in .env is incorrect, the database server is not running, or network/firewall issues.
problems.solution: Verify DATABASE_URL format: postgresql://user:password@host:port/dbname. Ensure the database server is running. Check that the database exists: npx prisma db push.

problems.problem: Multiple Prisma Client instances warning in development
problems.cause: Hot reloading creates a new Prisma Client on each reload, exhausting database connections.
problems.solution: Use the global singleton pattern: store the client on globalThis and reuse it. This prevents connection pool exhaustion during development.

problems.problem: Prisma query returns null for relations
problems.cause: Relations are not included by default in Prisma queries. You must explicitly request them.
problems.solution: Use include: { posts: true } or select: { posts: { select: { id: true, title: true } } } to load relations.

3.5.2.intuition: The Repository Pattern is like having a personal librarian. Instead of searching the stacks yourself (writing Prisma queries everywhere), you tell the librarian what you need (call repository methods). You can replace the librarian with a different one (swap implementations) and your reading habits (business logic) do not change.

exercises.title: Build a Repository Layer
exercises.description: Create repository classes for User and Post models with all CRUD operations. Define interfaces for each repository. Create both Prisma and in-memory implementations. Wire them into services.
exercises.hint: Define the interface first (IUserRepository), then implement PrismaUserRepository and InMemoryUserRepository. The service constructor should accept IUserRepository.

exercises.title: Test Service with Mock Repository
exercises.description: Write comprehensive unit tests for a UserService that uses an in-memory repository. Test: create user, duplicate email handling, find by ID (found and not found), update, and delete. No database needed.
exercises.hint: Instantiate InMemoryUserRepository in beforeEach and pass it to new UserService(repo). Tests run in memory with no database setup.

exercises.title: Generic Base Repository
exercises.description: Create a generic base repository class that implements common operations (findAll, findById, create, update, delete) for any Prisma model. Extend it for specific models that need custom queries.
exercises.hint: Use generics: BaseRepository<Model, CreateInput, UpdateInput>. Pass the Prisma delegate (prisma.user, prisma.post) to the constructor.

problems.problem: Repository adds boilerplate without clear benefit
problems.cause: The project is small and the abstraction layer feels like over-engineering.
problems.solution: For small projects, it is OK to skip the repository pattern and use Prisma directly in services. Adopt it when testability or data source swapping becomes a priority.

problems.problem: Repository interface does not match all Prisma query capabilities
problems.cause: Prisma offers rich filtering, including, and aggregation that is hard to abstract into a generic interface.
problems.solution: Do not try to abstract every Prisma feature. Use the repository for common CRUD and simple queries. For complex, one-off queries, it is acceptable to use Prisma directly in the service layer.

further_reading.title: Prisma Unit Testing Guide
further_reading.note: Official Prisma guide for mocking and testing

3.5.3.intuition: A connection pool is like a fleet of rental cars at an airport. Instead of buying a new car (opening a connection) every time someone lands (request arrives) and scrapping it when they leave (closing connection), you maintain a fleet (pool) of cars that travelers check out and return. The fleet size must match demand without exceeding parking capacity (database limits).

exercises.title: Optimize N+1 Queries
exercises.description: Given an Express endpoint that fetches posts and their authors using a loop (N+1 problem), refactor it to use Prisma's include for eager loading. Measure the query count before and after using Prisma's query logging.
exercises.hint: Enable query logging with PrismaClient({ log: ['query'] }). Replace the loop with findMany({ include: { author: true } }). Compare the number of SQL queries logged.

exercises.title: Connection Pool Health Monitor
exercises.description: Create a /health/db endpoint that checks database connectivity, measures query latency, and reports pool status. If query latency exceeds 500ms or the connection fails, return a 503 status.
exercises.hint: Use prisma.$queryRaw`SELECT 1` wrapped in timing logic. Return { status, latency, connected } in the response.

exercises.title: Query Performance Dashboard
exercises.description: Add Prisma query logging that tracks all queries over 100ms. Create an in-memory store of slow queries and expose them via a GET /debug/slow-queries endpoint (protected, development only).
exercises.hint: Use prisma.$on('query', ...) to capture query events. Store them in an array (limit to last 100). Filter by duration threshold.

problems.problem: 'Timed out fetching a new connection from the connection pool' error
problems.cause: All connections in the pool are in use. Requests are waiting longer than pool_timeout for an available connection.
problems.solution: Increase connection_limit in DATABASE_URL. Check for connection leaks (unawaited queries, long transactions). Reduce transaction scope. Consider using PgBouncer.

problems.problem: Database 'too many connections' error in production
problems.cause: Total connections across all application instances exceed the database's max_connections setting.
problems.solution: Calculate: num_instances * pool_size must be less than max_connections. Reduce pool_size per instance or increase database max_connections. Use PgBouncer for external pooling.

problems.problem: API responses are slow despite simple queries
problems.cause: N+1 query problem — loading relations in a loop instead of using eager loading or joins.
problems.solution: Use Prisma's include or select for eager loading. Enable query logging to identify N+1 patterns. Use Promise.all for independent parallel queries.

3.6.1.intuition: Building this Blog API is like constructing a building. The project structure is the blueprint, Prisma is the foundation (data layer), middleware is the building systems (plumbing, electrical), auth is the security system, and the route handlers are the rooms where things actually happen.

exercises.title: Complete the Blog API
exercises.description: Following the architecture outlined in this lesson, implement the full Blog API with all endpoints. Include: user registration/login with JWT, CRUD for posts with authorization (only authors can edit/delete their posts, admins can edit/delete any), comments on posts, and tag management.
exercises.hint: Start with the database schema and migrations. Then build auth module first (register, login). Then posts, then comments. Test each module before moving to the next.

exercises.title: Add Slug-Based Post Lookup
exercises.description: In addition to looking up posts by ID, support looking up by slug: GET /api/posts/slug/:slug. Ensure slugs are unique and generated from the title. Handle slug conflicts by appending a timestamp.
exercises.hint: Add a route before the :id route to avoid conflicts. Generate slugs with: title.toLowerCase().replace(/[^a-z0-9]+/g, '-') + '-' + Date.now().toString(36).

exercises.title: Add Post Publishing Workflow
exercises.description: Implement a publishing workflow: posts start as drafts. Add PATCH /api/posts/:id/publish and PATCH /api/posts/:id/unpublish endpoints. Only published posts appear in the public listing. Authors and admins can see drafts.
exercises.hint: Add a published boolean and publishedAt timestamp. Filter by published:true for public GET requests. Include drafts when the requesting user is the author.

problems.problem: Circular dependency between auth middleware and user service
problems.cause: Auth middleware imports user service, which imports auth utilities, creating a circular import.
problems.solution: Extract token verification into a standalone module that does not depend on any service. The auth middleware should only verify the token and set req.user from the payload — not fetch the full user from the database on every request.

problems.problem: Prisma migration fails with 'database does not exist'
problems.cause: The PostgreSQL database referenced in DATABASE_URL has not been created.
problems.solution: Create the database first: createdb myapp (or via psql: CREATE DATABASE myapp). Then run npx prisma migrate dev.

further_reading.title: Express REST API Best Practices
further_reading.note: Comprehensive Node.js best practices guide

further_reading.title: Building REST APIs with Express and Prisma

3.6.2.intuition: Pagination is like reading a book — you do not load the entire book into your brain at once; you read one page at a time. Filtering is the table of contents that lets you jump to relevant chapters. Search is the index at the back that points you to specific topics across all chapters.

exercises.title: Implement Both Pagination Styles
exercises.description: Build a posts endpoint that supports both offset (?page=2&limit=20) and cursor (?cursor=42&limit=20) pagination based on a ?mode=offset|cursor query parameter. Include complete metadata in responses.
exercises.hint: Check the mode query parameter. For offset, use skip/take. For cursor, use cursor/skip(1)/take(limit+1). Each mode returns different meta fields.

exercises.title: Multi-Tag Filtering
exercises.description: Implement tag filtering that supports both AND and OR logic: ?tags=node,express&tagMode=and (posts with ALL tags) vs ?tags=node,express&tagMode=or (posts with ANY tag).
exercises.hint: For AND: use tags: { every: { name: { in: tagArray } } } but note Prisma limitations. For OR: use tags: { some: { name: { in: tagArray } } }. For strict AND, you may need raw SQL.

exercises.title: Search with Ranking
exercises.description: Implement a search endpoint that ranks results by relevance. Title matches should rank higher than content matches. Use PostgreSQL full-text search with ts_rank, or implement a simple scoring system in JavaScript.
exercises.hint: For simple scoring: search both fields, assign score 10 for title match and 1 for content match, sort by score descending.

problems.problem: Pagination returns duplicate or missing items when data changes
problems.cause: Offset-based pagination shifts items when rows are inserted or deleted between page requests.
problems.solution: Use cursor-based pagination for data that changes frequently. Or use a consistent sort key (like ID) that does not change when new items are added.

problems.problem: Search is very slow on large tables
problems.cause: LIKE '%search%' queries cannot use standard B-tree indexes and require a full table scan.
problems.solution: Use PostgreSQL full-text search indexes (GIN index on tsvector columns). Or use a dedicated search engine like Elasticsearch or Meilisearch for complex search needs.

further_reading.title: Offset vs Cursor Pagination
further_reading.note: Slack engineering blog on pagination evolution

3.6.3.intuition: Testing is like a dress rehearsal before opening night. Supertest acts as a mock audience that watches every performance (endpoint), checking that actors (handlers) hit their marks (status codes), deliver their lines (response bodies), and handle hecklers (error cases) gracefully. Deployment prep is making sure the theater (production) has lighting, sound, and emergency exits ready.

exercises.title: Write Full Test Suite
exercises.description: Write integration tests for the Blog API covering: auth (register, login, invalid credentials), posts CRUD (create, read, update, delete with authorization), comments (create, delete), and edge cases (404, validation errors, unauthorized access). Aim for 80%+ coverage.
exercises.hint: Use beforeEach to clean the database and create test users. Use helper functions for authenticated requests. Test both happy paths and error cases.

exercises.title: Dockerize the Application
exercises.description: Create a Dockerfile using multi-stage builds for the Blog API. Include a docker-compose.yml with the API, PostgreSQL, and Redis services. Ensure migrations run on startup.
exercises.hint: Use node:20-alpine for small images. Copy only package*.json first for layer caching. Run prisma generate during build and prisma migrate deploy at startup.

exercises.title: CI/CD Pipeline
exercises.description: Create a GitHub Actions workflow that: checks out code, installs dependencies, runs type checking, linting, and tests with a PostgreSQL service container. Use matrix testing for Node 18 and 20.
exercises.hint: Use services: postgres in the workflow. Set DATABASE_URL as an environment variable. Run npx prisma migrate deploy before tests.

problems.problem: Tests interfere with each other — order-dependent failures
problems.cause: Tests share database state. A test that creates data affects subsequent tests.
problems.solution: Use beforeEach to clean the database (deleteMany on all tables in reverse dependency order). Or use transactions that roll back after each test.

problems.problem: Tests are slow due to bcrypt hashing
problems.cause: bcrypt with high cost factor (12) takes ~250ms per hash, slowing down tests that create users.
problems.solution: Use a lower bcrypt cost factor in tests: bcrypt.hash(password, 1). Set via environment variable: BCRYPT_ROUNDS=1 in test config.

problems.problem: Docker build fails with 'prisma generate' error
problems.cause: The Prisma schema or dependencies are not available during the Docker build step.
problems.solution: Copy prisma/ directory and package.json before running npm ci. Run npx prisma generate after npm ci. Ensure the .prisma/client directory is copied to the production stage.
